coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent sensor based on the reuse of a biological matrix for the visual detection of bilirubin
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Ying
Mao, Chunling
Xu, Hongzhan
Liang, Ning
Zhao, Longshan
coredata.dc:description: Bilirubin (Bil) is crucial for diagnosing jaundice and assessing liver function. Dynamic monitoring of Bil levels helps evaluate disease progression and therapy efficacy. Consequently, developing a method for rapid and accurate detection of bilirubin is essential for clinical diagnosis and therapeutic decision-making. With an emphasis on green and sustainable development, this study employed discarded peels of Dioscorea polystachya (yam) as a carbon source to create green carbon dots (CDs) using a one-step hydrothermal process. The preparation method was uncomplicated and energy-efficient without any specialized treatment. The prepared carbon dots (Dp-CDs) derived from the peel of yam exhibit high water solubility, remarkable photostability, and exceptional biocompatibility. Static quenching, the inner filter effect (IFE) and the aggregation quenching effect among the groups were the main causes of the decrease in fluorescence intensity of Dp-CDs produced by Bil. The fluorescence sensor constructed with the peel of yam presented a favourable response to Bil, with its fluorescence intensity showing a linear relationship with Bil concentration in the range of 0.30 and 200 μM and a detection limit of 0.28 μM. This method effectively identified and detected Bil in serum and urine in clinical practice, achieving recoveries between 95.6 % and 104.5 %. In addition, a paper-based test strip was designed for this experiment, characterized by its portability, economy, and simplicity of visualization. Dp-CDs provide a solid foundation for rapid and accurate Bil detection, which is critical for early diagnosis and illness monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dioscorea polystachya peels
Biological matrix
Carbon dots
Bilirubin detection
Green synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Bil by the Dp-CDs
Analysis of actual samples and comparison with related methods
Exploration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Dp-CDs by Bil
Evaluation of the greenness of the analytical method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of preparation and quenching mechanism of Dp-CDs.
(a) HRTEM picture of the Dp-CDs. The inset displayed the Dp-CDs' lattice spacing. (b) Particle size diagram of Dp-CDs (c) The FR-IR of Dp-CDs.
(a) XPS full spectrum diagram. (b) The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1 s, (c) N1 s, and (d) O1s.
(a) The UV-vis spectrum and the fluorescence spectrum of Dp-CDs. (b) Excitation-dependent features of Dp-CDs. (c) Photostability of Dp-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Dp-CDs at varying concentrations of Bil. (b) The Dp-CDs and Bil linear regression equation. (c) Images of Dp-CDs exposed to sunlight at varying concentrations of Bil. (d) Images of Dp-CDs exposed to UV light at several concentrations of Bil.

(a) Impact of various ions and amino acids on the fluorescence of Dp-CDs. (b) Influence of possible interfering substances on the fluorescence of the Dp-CDs. Stability of Dp-CDs at different (c) ionic strengths. (d) pH. (e) enzyme. (f) yam species.

(a) The diagram of fluorescence lifetime of Dp-CDs. (b) The Dp-CD fluorescence spectrum, with red indicating the excitation spectrum and blue denoting the emission spectrum. UV-vis spectrum of Bil (black). The shaded region denotes the overlap of the UV-vis spectra of Bil with the excitation spectrum of Dp-CDs. (c) Tauc diagram of Dp-CDs. (d) Mott-Schottky plots of Dp-CDs.
(a) Cyclic voltammetry diagram of Dp-CDs. (b) The energy level simulation diagram of Dp-CDs and Bil. (c) Fluorescence aggregation quenching effect between Dp-CDs and Bil.

(a) The cells viability of HT-22, BRL-3A and HuH-7 cells cultured with Dp-CDs for 72 h. (b)-(j) Microscopic image of cells. (b. HT-22 + 0 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 24 h; c. HT-22 + 100 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 24 h; d. HT-22 + 100 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 72 h. e. BRL-3A + 0 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 24 h; f. BRL-3A + 100 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 24 h; g. BRL-3A + 100 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 72 h. h. HuH-7 + 0 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 24 h; i. HuH-7 + 100 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 24 h; j. HuH-7 + 100 µg/mL Dp-CDs cultured for 72 h.

(a) Photos of paper-based test strip soaked in Dp-CDs and quenched by Bil solutions of different concentrations were observed under daylight. (b) Photos of paper-based test strip soaked in Dp-CDs and quenched by Bil solutions of different concentrations were observed under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp.
Detection of Bil in human serum and urine.
Comparison between Dp-CDs and other reported Bil analysis methods

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent sensor based on the reuse of a biological matrix for the visual detection of bilirubin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, Shenyang Liaoning Province, 110016, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Pharmacy, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.
Corresponding author at: School of Pharmaceutical Engineering, Shenyang Pharmaceutical University, 103 Wenhua Road Shenhe District, 110016, Shenyang, Liaoning Province, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bilirubin (Bil) is crucial for diagnosing jaundice and assessing liver function. Dynamic monitoring of Bil levels helps evaluate disease progression and therapy efficacy. Consequently, developing a method for rapid and accurate detection of bilirubin is essential for clinical diagnosis and therapeutic decision-making. With an emphasis on green and sustainable development, this study employed discarded peels of Dioscorea polystachya (yam) as a carbon source to create green carbon dots (CDs) using a one-step hydrothermal process. The preparation method was uncomplicated and energy-efficient without any specialized treatment. The prepared carbon dots (Dp-CDs) derived from the peel of yam exhibit high water solubility, remarkable photostability, and exceptional biocompatibility. Static quenching, the inner filter effect (IFE) and the aggregation quenching effect among the groups were the main causes of the decrease in fluorescence intensity of Dp-CDs produced by Bil. The fluorescence sensor constructed with the peel of yam presented a favourable response to Bil, with its fluorescence intensity showing a linear relationship with Bil concentration in the range of 0.30 and 200 μM and a detection limit of 0.28 μM. This method effectively identified and detected Bil in serum and urine in clinical practice, achieving recoveries between 95.6 % and 104.5 %. In addition, a paper-based test strip was designed for this experiment, characterized by its portability, economy, and simplicity of visualization. Dp-CDs provide a solid foundation for rapid and accurate Bil detection, which is critical for early diagnosis and illness monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Bilirubin (Bil) is a significant metabolic product of iron porphyrin compounds in the body, characterized as an orange-yellow pigment [1,2]. Bil serves as a critical clinical parameter for diagnosing jaundice [3]. The standard serum Bil level is 17.1 μM, with clinical jaundice occurring if it exceeds 34.2 μM [4]. High blood Bil concentrations can harm the central nervous system, and neonatal Bil levels usually don't go above 119.7 μM. Although neonatal jaundice is a common condition, most babies with it do not require treatment and will recover to normal levels in four weeks [5]. Nevertheless, approximately ten percent of neonates exhibit high levels of Bil (>205.2 μM) and slowly drop (>4 weeks), which may result in significant harm to cerebral development if not treated [6]. As a result, it is essential to assess Bil levels in all neonates and to track the fluctuations in Bil levels among patients with neonatal jaundice [7].

All existing technologies for Bil detection possess significant limitations, despite the utility of traditional techniques in clinical practice [8]. Clinical Bil detection now employs the diazo technique and necessitates venous blood collection. However, this approach is inappropriate for screening and dynamic assessment due to factors like the difficulties of blood collection in neonates, invasiveness, high expense, and the requirement for hospital testing [9]. The bilirubin oxidase (BOD) method enables specific oxidation of bilirubin (Bil) with high selectivity. However, BOD activity is highly sensitive to ambient temperature and pH, and improper storage conditions may reduce enzymatic efficiency, compromising detection accuracy [10]. High-performance liquid chromatography requires costly equipment and professional technicians, resulting in a significantly prolonged detection time. It is unsuitable for the clinical requirement of rapid detection. Consequently, an efficient, non-invasive, accurate, economical, and broadly applicable approach to Bil detection is essential. Additionally, developments in materials science offer a fresh theoretical framework and line of inquiry for this field of study.

CDs, an emerging nanomaterial, have demonstrated significant potential in biological detection in recent years. CDs exhibit a low production cost and a large surface area attributable to their diminutive dimensions. Consequently, they are easily modifiable and exhibit unique optical properties, enabling CDs to be used as a sensitive signal conversion element in biomolecule detection [11–13]. In addition, during the process of bioanalysis, sample preparation is a crucial step, especially when dealing with biological matrices. Biological matrices include various types such as blood, plasma, serum, urine, saliva, sweat, and tissues. Each type has its unique components and properties, which pose diverse requirements for sample preparation methods [14].

With the gradual promotion of the green chemistry concept, the raw materials of CDs are gradually replaced by natural organic matter, biomass waste, and even some simple carbon sources. This approach realizes the resource utilization of waste, reduces costs, and is both green and environmentally friendly [15]. Dioscorea polystachya (commonly known as yam) is a plant with a homology of medicine and food [16]. Yam processing generates substantial peel waste, resulting in resource wastage and potential environmental pressure. These discarded peels, rich in abundant carbon sources (polysaccharide, cellulose) and bioactive compounds, serve as sustainable precursors for CD synthesis. This study utilizes the discarded peel of yam as a carbon source to synthesize Dp-CDs for the quantitative detection of Bil (Scheme 1 ). On the one hand, the prepared Dp-CDs have excellent optical properties and biocompatibility. On the other hand, fluorescence quenching results from static quenching, the inner filter effect and the interaction between Dp-CDs and Bil groups. This approach exhibits greater sensitivity and specificity. The constructed detection system shows a good linear response range to Bil. Furthermore, real-time, on-site Bil detection enables rapid identification of jaundice. For newborns, timely diagnosis and treatment of jaundice are critically important. Rapid on-site Bil quantification can also monitor therapeutic efficacy in real time, allowing clinicians to assess whether treatment is progressing favorably by tracking dynamic changes in Bil levels. To address this need, we developed a paper-based test strip that facilitates simple, rapid, and visual Bil detection, offering a practical tool to enhance jaundice management. In summary, this study proposes a novel strategy with significant potential for clinical diagnosis and biomedical research applications.
Details were provided in the Supplementary data files.

Dp-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method with the peel of yam as the precursor. Initially, the peel of yam was dried and then crushed into a powder. 1.5 g of yam peel powder was dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water and transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, heated at 200 °C for 6 h under a pressure of 1.55 MPa, without requiring additional pressurization equipment. After cooling, the sample was transferred to a 50 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 4200 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant obtained after centrifugation was filtered through a microporous filter membrane (0.22 μm) to get the brownish-yellow Dp-CDs stock solution for subsequent experiments.
Details were provided in the supplementary data files.
Details were provided in the supplementary data files.

Took a circular filter with a diameter of 3 cm, soaked it in the Dp-CDs solution for 24 h, and then dried it. To achieve visualization, drop Bil solutions of different concentrations (0–280 μM) onto the dried filter paper and irradiate it under a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp. The prepared paper-based test strips can be stored in a sealed condition at 4 °C for future use.

The structural characterization of CDs, a novel nanomaterial, is crucial for thoroughly comprehending their characteristics and potential uses. In this work, the dimensions, morphology, and lattice structure of Dp-CDs were systematically analyzed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM) to establish robust structure-property relationships. Dp-CDs have a roughly spherical nanoparticle shape, uniform size, and high dispersion, as seen in Fig. 1 a and b. Dp-CDs have an average diameter of 3.0 ± 0.82 nm (N = 35). Dp-CD particle sizes exhibit a normal distribution, with most falling between 2 and 4 nm. The lattice spacing is 0.20 nm, the same as the graphite (100) plane [17]. FT-IR analysis of Dp-CDs was used to determine which functional groups were present. Fig. 1c illustrates a strong and wide peak at 3242 cm-1, ascribed to the OH/NH stretching vibration [18]. The absorption band at 2929 cm⁻¹ arises from CH stretching vibrations of the methylene group. A distinct peak at 1664 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the C = O stretching vibration [19]. The characteristic absorption peaks at 1585 cm-1 and 1392 cm-1 were identified as the stretching vibration peaks of NH and CN bonds, which can be attributed to amide bonds [20]. The stretching vibrations of CO-C and CO are represented by the distinctive absorption peaks at 1200 and 1069 cm-1 [21]. When carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids are broken down during hydrothermal processes, amino, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups may be produced [22].

The surface elemental composition and chemical states of Dp-CDs were analyzed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) revealed three distinct peaks at 285.1 eV (C 1 s), 400.8 eV (N 1 s), and 532.1 eV (O 1 s), corresponding to carbon (70.06 %), nitrogen (5.13 %), and oxygen (24.81 %), respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) was deconvoluted into three distinct binding energy peaks at 284.9 eV (C–C/C = C), 286.4 eV (C–N/C–O), and 288.0 eV (C = O), confirming the coexistence of graphitic carbon frameworks and oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups [22]. Two fitted peaks in the N1 s spectra (Fig. 2c) are located at 399.7 eV and 400.9 eV, respectively, and match pyrrole-N and graphite-N [23]. This favors the provision of active sites in the reaction and facilitates electron migration [24,25]. The C = O and COH groups are represented by the two peaks in the O1 s spectra (Fig. 2d), located at 531.2 eV and 532.3 eV. The nitrogen- and oxygen-containing functional groups in Dp-CDs substantially enhance their hydrophilicity. This stabilization of the Dp-CDs’ active sites in aqueous environments promotes the formation of an energy gap, thereby improving fluorescence performance [26].

The photophysical properties of Dp-CDs, including absorption and emission behaviors, were systematically characterized using ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy to elucidate their luminescence mechanisms and sensing application potential. There is a noticeable shoulder peak at 278 nm in the UV–Vis spectra, as seen in Fig. 3 a. The n-π* transition of the C = O bond is responsible for this feature of CDs [26,27]. Furthermore, Dp-CDs exhibit unique excitation-dependent photoluminescence (Fig. 3b), where emission wavelengths shift systematically with variations in the excitation wavelength [28,29]. This phenomenon may be attributed to factors such as the size, surface state, internal structure, and surrounding environment of the CDs. The emission wavelength of Dp-CDs exhibits a redshift as the excitation wavelength increases from 400 nm to 520 nm. Multiple investigations indicate that the maximum emission and excitation wavelengths of Dp-CDs are 513 nm and 465 nm, respectively. Based on these findings, 465 nm was selected as the optimal excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments. Upon exposure to UV light (λ = 365 nm), the Dp-CD aqueous solution exhibited strong green fluorescence, appearing light yellow during daytime conditions. The calculated absolute quantum yield (QY) of 4.0 % for Dp-CDs in aqueous solution validated the reaction. The fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared Dp-CDs remained essentially constant after 60 min of Xe-lamp irradiation (Fig. 3c), indicating that the Dp-CDs showed good photostability.
Details were provided in the supplementary data files.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Bil by the Dp-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal experimental conditions, distinct variations in fluorescence intensity can be readily observed when monitoring bilirubin (Bil) concentrations using a Dp-CD-based fluorescent sensor under 465 nm excitation. As Fig. 4 a illustrates, Dp-CDs responded well to Bil, with fluorescence intensity following a consistent downward trend as Bil concentration increased within the 0–350 μM concentration range. It was clear that the color of the Dp-CDs+Bil system gradually deepened when exposed to daylight (Fig. 4c), while its fluorescence intensity gradually dropped when exposed to UV lamp radiation (Fig. 4d). Furthermore, a slight redshift in the emission wavelength was observed with increasing Bil concentration. This phenomenon is likely attributed to the reduced intermolecular distance and enhanced interactions at higher Bil concentrations, collectively leading to a redshift in the emission spectrum. Fig. 4b shows the linear relationship between the variations in fluorescence intensity values of Dp-CDs at various Bil concentrations. An excellent linear relationship between the concentration of Bil and the degree of Dp-CD fluorescence attenuation may be observed by plotting F/F0 vs. Bil concentration in the range of 0.30–200 μM. The linear regression equation is expressed as F/F0 = −0.003 CBil + 0.955 (F represents fluorescence value adding Bil, and F0 denotes the fluorescence value without Bil), with a correlation coefficient (R²) of 0.9952. The calculated detection limit, using the formula 3σ/k (σ represents the standard deviation of 11 pieces of blank sample data and k denotes the slope of the standard curve), is 0.28 μM.

To mitigate the impact of possible interfering substances on the outcomes of the experiment, this study selected 9 types of ions (K+, Na+, Ba2+, Al3+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, HCO3 -), 9 common amino acids (Ser, His, Phe, Gln, Cys, Val, Glu, Arg, Thr), and 25 types of biological molecules (Glucose, Fructose, Maltose, Sucrose, Thiourea, Creatinine, Uric, Creatine, Vitamin C, Urea, Amylum, Biotin, Glutathione, Galactose, Dopamine, Vitamin D, Serum albumin, Coproporphyrin, Vitamin B2, Quinine, Tetracyclines, Sulfonamides, Catechins, Resveratrol, Malondialdehyde) that may cause interference to assess the selectivity of Dp-CDs for Bil, utilizing deionized water as a control. In this experiment, the concentration of potential interferents introduced was tenfold that of Bil. Fig. 5 a and b demonstrate that the addition of Bil is the only thing that causes the fluorescence of Dp-CDs to be quenched. In contrast, other potential interfering substances do not significantly alter the fluorescence intensity, even at concentrations ten times greater than Bil. To further validate the specificity of Dp-CDs for Bil detection, we evaluated the fluorescence response of the Dp-CDs+Bil system in the presence of these interferents. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the Dp-CDs+Bil complex remained unchanged, confirming the high selectivity of the sensor.

To demonstrate the potential of Dp-CDs for practical applications, we systematically investigated their stability under varying salt concentrations, different pH, and exposure to diverse enzymes. A series of NaCl solutions with varying concentrations was prepared to examine the salt stability on Dp-CDs. The data presented in Fig. 5c suggests that variations in NaCl concentration exert no significant effect on the fluorescence value, indicating that the Dp-CDs exhibit salt tolerance. The pH of the system was altered to investigate the variations in fluorescence intensity of Dp-CDs across the range of pH 1 to 13. Fig. 5d indicates that Dp-CDs exhibit stability within the pH range of 3 to 11, accompanied by a minor variation in fluorescence intensity. Under extreme acid-base conditions, the fluorescence value of Dp-CDs decreases rapidly. This phenomenon occurs because the functional groups on the surface of CDs that readily undergo protonation and deprotonation in strongly acidic and alkaline conditions, thereby modifying the surface charge distribution and chemical characteristics of the Dp-CDs, which impacts their stability. Consequently, pH = 7 was selected for the experiment due to its convenience. As shown in Fig. 5e, the fluorescence intensity of Dp-CDs exhibited no significant changes in the presence of biologically relevant enzymes (protease, lipase, amylase, and lysozyme), further supporting their stability in complex biological environments. To validate the universal applicability of the raw materials, we further investigated the influence of different yam peel varieties—Iron yam, Common yam rhizome and Fine hair yam—on the fluorescence properties of the synthesized Dp-CDs. Experimental results (Fig. 7f) demonstrated that variations in yam peel varieties caused no interference with the fluorescence intensity of Dp-CDs. The prepared Dp-CDs were stored appropriately (4 °C refrigerator), and the fluorescence intensity was observed to maintain 92.7 % of the initial value when tested daily over 15 consecutive days. It may be related to the precursor material of CDs being a biological matrix (the peel of yam). The results demonstrate that Dp-CDs exhibit significant stability and applicability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of actual samples and comparison with related methods
ce:section.ce:para: This study demonstrated the practical utility of the fluorescence sensor by analyzing Bil in human serum and urine samples. Since no Bil was detected in the original samples, the standard addition method was employed to quantify Bil in spiked matrices. Bil solutions at low, medium, and high concentrations (50, 100, and 150 μM) were added to two sets of samples for testing. The recovery and RSD (relative standard deviation) of Bil are presented in the subsequent Table 1 , indicating recoveries ranging from 95.6 % to 104.5 %, with RSD below 0.91 %. The findings indicated that the Dp-CDs fluorescence probe offered new opportunities for the clinical detection of Bil. In contrast to prior research, as demonstrated in Table S1, the current work developed a fluorescent sensor for Bil detection utilizing the peel of yam as a carbon source, with an expanded sensing range and reduced detection limit. Additionally, Dp-CDs were synthesized employing the hydrothermal technique utilizing discarded peel of yam as a precursor, representing a straightforward and cost-effective preparation process that does not produce additional by-products, adhering to the principles of environmental sustainability. Consequently, Dp-CDs fluorescence probes may swiftly, sensitively and environmentally detect Bil, presenting significant potential for clinical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Dp-CDs by Bil
ce:section.ce:para: This work elucidates the theory of fluorescence quenching of Dp-CDs by Bil by analyzing fluorescence lifetime (PL), UV-visible spectroscopy, chemical groups, and energy level modeling. The study of fluorescence lifetime is an effective method of distinguishing between dynamic and static quenching. The fluorescence lifetimes of Dp-CDs and Dp-CDs + Bil systems were fitted, and their average fluorescence lifetimes were calculated. Fig. 6 a displayed that the fluorescence lifetime of Dp-CDs before adding the test substance was 1.21 ns, with no significant change observed after adding Bil, which suggested that fluorescence resonance energy transfer does not transpire between Dp-CDs and Bil and that the fluorescence quenching of Dp-CDs by Bil may result from static quenching. The quenching mechanism was further elucidated based on the alterations in the UV–Vis spectra of Dp-CDs before and after Bil addition. As shown in Fig. 3a, the emergence of new absorption peaks in the UV–Vis spectrum of Dp-CDs upon Bil interaction suggests the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex. This complex formation could lead to a new electron transition, thereby demonstrating that the quenching mechanism is static quenching. Furthermore, Fig. 6b illustrates that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Bil coincides with the excitation spectrum of Dp-CDs. The excitation light irradiates the sample, causing the molecules of the measured substance in the overlapping region to absorb the excitation light. This absorption reduces the intensity of the excitation light reaching the fluorescent substance, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity. The quenching of Dp-CDs by Bil is confirmed to result from the inner filter effect.

Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) is a contributing factor to fluorescence quenching. To verify the occurrence of electron transfer during the experimental procedure, this work employed Mott-Schottky curves (M-S) and cyclic voltammetry (CV curves) to evaluate the electrochemical properties of Dp-CDs. Fig. 6c illustrates that the Tauc diagram was plotted using the UV–Vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS) of Dp-CDs, resulting in a band gap (Eg) of 3.07 eV. The flat-band potentials of Dp-CDs were explored using M-S curves to demonstrate the electron transfer mechanism of Dp-CDs. The M-S curve (Fig. 6d) indicates that the flat-band potential of the Dp-CDs is −0.16 eV. The positive slope of the tangent line of the M-S curve signifies that the semiconductor material is n-type, with a conduction-band potential of 0.20 eV lower than the flat-band potential [30]. From this, the value of the conduction-band potential of Dp-CDs can be calculated to be −0.36 eV. Combined with the Eg obtained from the UV–Vis DRS measurements described above. According to the equation:
(1)
E VB
=
E CB
+
E g
(where EVB is the valence band potential, ECB denotes the conduction band potential, and Eg represents the band gap energy.)

The calculated EVB value of Dp-CDs was 2.71 eV. Therefore, the potential electron transfer path of Dp-CDs can be deduced. We aim to investigate the relationship between the Bil quenching mechanism of Dp-CDs and the photoinduced electron transfer. This work calculated the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of Dp-CDs using CV curves and the subsequent equations [31]. Ered and Eox represent the reduction potential and oxidation potential of Dp-CDs, respectively.
(2)
ELUMO = − e
(
E red
+ 4
. 4
)
(3)
EHOMO = − e
(
E ox
+ 4
. 4
)

The reduction potential (Ered) of Dp-CDs was ascertained by analyzing the CV curve (Fig. 7 a). The ELUMO, according to formula (2), was −4.28 eV. Due to the irreversibility of the oxidation process, it is impossible to calculate EHOMO using a formula (3) [32]. The band gap width Eg was calculated from the Tauc plot (Fig. 6c), and the EHOMO value was computed to be −7.35 eV using Eq. (4).
(4)
E HOMO
=
E LUMO
− −
E g

Meanwhile, the EHOMO and ELUMO values of Bil can be calculated as −5.39 eV and −2.12 eV, respectively, according to Gaussian software. Fig. 7b illustrates that the excited state electrons of Dp-CDs cannot transition from the LUMO energy level to the LUMO energy level of Bil, confirming that photoinduced electron transfer is not responsible for the fluorescence quenching.

Building on these findings, our study investigated the fluorescence quenching mechanism by analyzing the chemical interactions between Dp-CDs and Bil. The previously mentioned XPS and FT-IR results indicate that Dp-CDs possess many hydrophilic groups on their surface, including -OH and -NH2. Bil possesses a complex and distinctive structure, featuring substituent groups including hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl groups. The chemical groups of both give rise to interactions (hydrogen bonding), forming a stable aggregate structure that leads to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) of Dp-CDs (Fig. 7c).

Biomaterials must possess excellent biocompatibility to guarantee the safety of clinical applications. We assessed the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of Dp-CDs by testing the survival of HT-22, BRL-3A, and HuH-7 cells exposed to varying doses (0–100 μg/mL) of Dp-CDs with the CCK8. As shown in Fig. 8 a, when HT-22, BRL-3A, and HuH-7 cells were incubated with Dp-CDs for 72 h, their viability slightly decreased with increasing concentrations of Dp-CDs. Notably, even at the highest concentration of Dp-CDs (100 μg/mL), the survival rates of HT-22, BRL-3A, and HuH-7 cells remained above 90 %. Fig. 8b-g illustrates the fluorescent inverted microscope images before and after the addition of Dp-CDs, as well as following prolonged incubation (72 h) with Dp-CDs, demonstrating that the cell count and morphology of the three cell types remained unaltered. Dp-CDs can promote effective adhesion and stretching of cells, suggesting that Dp-CDs synthesized with the peel of yam as a precursor exhibit low cytotoxicity and could coexist harmoniously with the cells, showing superior biocompatibility. Even with extended incubation time, no adverse effects on the cells were observed. Consequently, the Dp-CDs synthesized in this experiment provide new development prospects in clinical detection and may significantly influence the future pharmaceutical field.

This experiment involved the design of a paper-based test strip for the practicability of Dp-CDs. The filter paper strip was submerged in a Dp-CDs solution for 24 h, utilizing adsorption and capillary action, and subsequently dried. Various concentrations of Bil (0–280 μM) solutions were dropped on the filter paper, allowing the filter paper to interact with the Bil upon contact. Fig. 9 a illustrates that in sunlight, a rise in Bil concentration results in the color of the filter paper gradually deepening. Fig. 9b illustrates that under UV lamp (365 nm) irradiation, an increase in Bil concentration results in a progressive darkening of the filter paper, signifying that Bil quenches the fluorescence of Dp-CDs, leading to a reduction in fluorescence intensity. Additionally, paper-based test strips immersed in the Dp-CDs solution for 24 h were dried and stored at 4 °C for 15 days before being used for Bil detection. Fig S2, the test strips stored for 15 days remained capable of effectively detecting Bil, with detection performance comparable to that of freshly prepared strips. This result confirms the long-term stability and practical utility of the Dp-CDs paper-based detection system. The straightforward paper-based visualization determination provided intuitive and powerful evidence for our experiments. Moreover, the paper-based visualization determination is a practical and economical method for effectively detecting Bil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the greenness of the analytical method
ce:section.ce:para: Environmental protection profile tools and techniques greenness assessment have been widely employed in sample preparation and the creation of analytical methods in recent years due to the ongoing development of the idea of green chemistry [33]. This study reflects green characteristics in several aspects. To more accurately assess the greenness of this method, we employed the AGREE, AGREEprep, and MoGAPI tools for a multi-faceted assessment [34,35]. A comparison between this method and previously reported methods (Table 2 ) clearly demonstrates that the fluorescence method yields higher scores and more green indicators. In this experiment, the utilization of biomass waste as raw material achieves resource recycling, thereby reducing dependence on new resources, which aligns with the requirement for feedstock sustainability in green chemistry. During the synthesis of Dp-CDs, a one-step hydrothermal method was employed, eliminating the need for complicated equipment or substantial chemical reagents. This approach effectively reduces energy consumption and minimizes the generation of chemical waste. Additionally, the detection process is relatively simple, requiring no complex pre-treatment steps. It enables rapid acquisition of results and improves detection efficiency. In contrast, the HPLC system generates approximately 0.5 Ls of organic solvent waste per day, involves intricate pre-treatment procedures, and its operation and maintenance consume substantial energy and resources. While electrochemical sensing demonstrates high sensitivity for bilirubin detection, its sample preparation is cumbersome, requiring the fabrication of electrode chips, drying, and analysis via electrochemical workstations, which increases operational complexity and time costs. Consequently, evaluations using AGREE, AGREEprep, and MoGAPI tools reveal that fluorescent sensors for bilirubin detection exhibit distinct green advantages, higher overall sustainability metrics, and significant potential for sustainable development, making them promising for broader applications in future bio-detection fields. However, this study still has limitations requiring improvement. Compared to emerging nanomaterial-based detection methods, there remains room for enhancement in detection sensitivity. In summary, the fluorescence method demonstrates more evident green advantages over HPLC and electrochemical approaches, minimizing the environmental harm caused by conventional analytical methods and contributing to sustainable development goals.

This study effectively synthesized Dp-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method using discarded yam peel as a carbon source for quantitatively detecting Bil in clinical applications. The technique is ecologically sustainable, economically efficient, and uncomplicated, aligning with the principles of green chemistry. The prepared Dp-CDs exhibited uniform particle size, excellent dispersion, and robust stability. In addition, Dp-CDs contain abundant functional groups that augment hydrophilicity and improve solubility and fluorescence intensity. The low toxicity and favourable biocompatibility of Dp-CDs, prepared with a biological matrix, were verified by the CCK8 approach, making them appropriate for sensing applications. Simultaneously, static quenching, the internal filtration effect and the interaction among functional groups achieved the rapid and sensitive detection of Bil. The sensor demonstrated robust anti-interference capability, a broad linear range, and a low detection limit. To enhance practicality, we designed a paper-based test strip for facile visual Bil detection. This experiment, aligned with green, renewable, and sustainable development principles, demonstrated the feasibility of synthesizing Dp-CDs from discarded yam peel. This innovation offers a novel approach to the clinical detection of Bil, utilizing CDs derived from biological matrices as precursors, presenting significant potential for diverse applications.

Ying Yan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Chunling Mao: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Hongzhan Xu: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Ning Liang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Longshan Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Liaoning Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (2024-MS-088), the Youqing lift Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (YQ202206) and the Middle-aged Backbone Personnel Training Program of Shenyang Pharmaceutical University (ZQN2016011).
